speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 7, the final tape of the interview of Mr. Drexel Sprecher on March 24th, 1996 in Chevy Chase, Maryland. And Mr Sprecher is here with some members of his family. I'm still Bruce Einhorn. But more to the point, Mr. Sprecher, why don't you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to my right is Virginia Lee Sprecher, who has been my good wife since September, nineteen hundred and?

speaker: interviewee
text: '49. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: '49. Right. And next to her is Drexel A. Sprecher Jr., who came along about a year and a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Half.

speaker: interviewee
text: A half? After marriage. And then we had another daughter, Jenna Sprecher, Jenna Garmon Sprecher, who is now in Florida. And then we had, as our third, and last, and most beautiful child, Karen. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Malloy Sprecher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Karen Malloy Sprecher-- Malloy, being my mother's maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Karen is-- is our lady seated to the far right here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, it's a lovely family. Thank you all, for letting us share your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: --didn't talk very often, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, lay off the sauce till they're through. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, as we conclude now, does anybody have anything which he or she would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bruce, I've thought a great deal about the impact that my father's Nuremberg experience had on me. My father often talked about the trials, even when I was a very young child. And in these conversations, he taught me the importance of freedom, of justice, of the right of each person to develop their full potential. Most importantly, he instilled in me the idea that public service was the highest calling. And as an adult, I have dedicated myself to discovering a new political philosophy for the renewal of our American civilization, and for extending our contribution to the world. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in this search, I see wider horizons, because I've been privileged to stand on the shoulders of giants. I now hold the Nuremberg Trials and what my father taught me in a larger historical context. The trials began to institutionalize the idea that reason could triumph over force, a process which continues today all over the world. And in this-- in this process-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] in this process, there is-- I no longer-- this process no longer-- this no longer appears to be the summit of human evolution. But I consider it a very important step, a very important step in the larger historical process of human consciousness, reshaping the world to realize our highest ideals. And for that, we owe my father and his colleagues at Nuremberg our enduring gratitude.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Drex. Karen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking a lot this weekend, through the reunion, of how Nuremberg has touched all of our lives, and the personal ends of it. I think my father, upon returning to the US, formed a camaraderie with his fellow prosecutors that was a close one, because they had been through such horror. Certainly, one lesson I've learned as I have scratched the surface of information regarding the Holocaust and the Nuremberg Trials is abject horror at the kind of evil of which man is capable. I think that the legacy that I try to ensure will be carried on is to bear witness to the horrors of what Hitler and his colleagues did and hope that as-- actually Chris Dodd, on Friday, so inspirationally stated-- we haven't yet succeeded, if we can still have things occurring in the world such as the atrocities in Iraq, and today in Bosnia, and last year in Rwanda. We're not finished yet. We have to remember what happened and carry on. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERS) Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks to all of you, very, very much. It was a privilege.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Bruce.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You've been under that hot light all afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- OK. Dexter, can you tell me what this picture is all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's a family picture taken, I would think, about-- in the early 1920s. And it shows my family, my father, and a-- a number of my aunts and so on. Of course, my mother is not there, because she had been killed in an automobile accident some years before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what this picture is all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father and I were fishing for lake trout up in the Lake of the Woods in Canada. And we had caught some lake trout there, by trolling at a very deep level, 200, 300 feet beneath the surface. And those trout, lake trout, probably weighed 21 and 23 pounds, if I remember rightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That would have been about 1935, '36, '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are these two photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those are pictures of Virginia and me at our wedding, in September of 1949. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember that. We got married in Baltimore and had quite a-- a bunch of people from the families and friends of both sides in attendance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture all about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of Virginia and me together with a person who is very important in the lives of our family and in my life in Nuremberg. Hans Wilson, who's on the right, was a former German journalist who had fled to England after the Nazis came to power. And later on, I managed to recruit him as my principal research analyst. And later, after I returned to the States and he became an American citizen, he became a very close friend of my family. And that's where you see him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where was this picture shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that picture was shot in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Daddy, that's actually at Joe DuBois' house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my god. Yes, you're right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another Nuremberger.

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture-- this picture was taken at the home of Joe DuBois, another Nuremberg prosecutor, in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that would be about 1950--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Daddy, because this is-- I was in my teens. I think we're talking 1975, I bet.

speaker: interviewee
text: You think it's that late?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that sound right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't we look at the-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, what is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph shows Mr. Justice Robert Jackson, the chief American prosecutor, making a statement to the tribunal. And behind him is, on-- to the left-- that's to his right-- is Tom Dodd, who had become the executive trial counsel. And then, a number of the rest of us are scattered around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, of whom is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photograph is of General Telford Taylor, who had been promoted to Brigadier General and had become the successor to Mr. Justice Jackson as the Chief of Council for War Crimes for the United States of America. And that was the picture taken of him, as he was about to make a presentation to the tribunal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph shows mainly the prosecution tables, or several of them, which were on the right side of the courtroom. To the right, you begin to see the press behind the barrier. And the photograph shows Tom Dodd, who's the Executive Chief Counsel, and Whitney Harris, who's one of-- the assistant trial counsel, Dr. Kempner, who is assistant trial counsel, and then some of the other officials of the prosecution. And on the far side, are some of the assistants to the tribunal, on the last table, far-- on the far side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- was taken just outside the courthouse of approximately one half of the principal defense council for the defendants. This is Dr. Sauter on the right side, and-- who is a counsel for the defendant, Von Shirach and Von Ribbentrop for a time. And in the second row is-- in the third row is Dr. Fritz, who represented the defendant, Fritzsche, whom I prosecuted. And other counsel that are there represented different leading defendants.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photograph of the defendant, Herman Goering, during an intermission, during his testimony on his own behalf in the main courtroom. And of course, there were always some American guards stationed around, just to be certain that none of the defendants misbehaved. And actually, there was very good behavior on the part of the defendants in the courtroom, without any great demonstrations, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of a part of the central city, the old city of Nuremberg, which shows the destruction caused by the bombing and shelling during the war. And the river running through it is the Pegnitz River. And if you were to go up that river toward its source, another 15 miles, you'd find out that this is some of the best trout fishing water in all of Germany. Goering used to fish trout there, and frankly, so did I, but some years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, Drex?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photograph shows me making the presentation against the defendant, Hans Fritzsche, to the tribunal. To the right of the picture is Sir David Maxwell Fyfe, holding his head with his arm, who is the acting chief prosecutor for the British. And to my left is General Rudenko-- it's actually to my right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go by the direction on the monitor, on the TV.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, to the-- to the left is General Rudenko, who is the chief British prosecutor. And just beside me is Lieutenant [CLEARS THROAT] Fred Neibergall, who was-- later, became head of the translation division and was a former German, who was very conversant in both English and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said General Rudenko was with whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but with what side? What government?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: He represented whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, General Rudenko was the chief Soviet prosecutor. Oh, my God, there's three defendants. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, Drex?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of our three children, Virginia's and mine. And to the left is Karen. And in the center is Drexel Jr.. And to the right is Jenna. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about 1957. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph, Drexel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is one that shows Virginia and me with our daughter, Jenna, and her husband, Gabe [? Venero. ?] And in Virginia's lap is our only grandson, whose name is Drexel Alejandro [? Venero. ?]
